rulings.Case Title: IN RE: WIKIMEDIA FOUNDATION, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion On Petition To Quash Subpoena
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, February 6, 2015, Line 3, PETITIONERS WIKIMEDIA FOUNDATION, INC. and JOHN DOE'S Petition To Quash Subpoena. Pro Tem Judge Katherine Huibonhoa, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: No tentative ruling has been issued. The parties are to appear. Each party is required to bring a proposed order to the hearing. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CANDLESTICK VIEW HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION, VS. J.L. BUILDERS, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, February 6, 2015, Line 3, DEFENDANT HMI ENTERPRISES INC., DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. The court overrules the demurrer. The court defers to plaintiff's construction of the prime contract. See Aragon-Haas v. FamilySecurity Ins. Services, Inc., (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 232, 239 ("While plaintiffs' interpretation of the contract may prove invalid, it [is] improper to resolve the issue against her solely on her own pleading. 'In ruling on a demurrer, the likelihood that the pleading will be able to prove his allegations is not the question.'"). Art. 27 of the prime contract indicates that the plaintiff could not compel the subcontractors to arbitrate. The other cited sections of the prime contract (Arts. 1.10, 1.12 and 29.1) do not clearly mandate a contrary result. At most, there is a disputed factual issue regarding the meaning of the prime contract and defendant's subcontract and the intent of the parties. Those issues cannot be adjudicated on demurrer, especially since the court does not have a copy of defendant's subcontract. Accordingly, defendant has not shown that its negligence was something litigable in the prior arbitration and res judicata applies. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: LEILA GOWEN VS. BRYAN LAMB et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, February 6, 2015, Line 4, DEFENDANT BRYAN LAMB MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND. Denied. The time for bringing a motion pursuant to CCP § 473(b) has expired. Thus, the Court may only set aside the default judgment by using its powers of equity if Defendant establishes "extrinsic fraud or mistake." (Aldrich v. San Fernando Valley Lumber Co. (1985) 170 Cal.App.3d 725, 738.) Defendant's "own negligence in following up and pursuing his case" should also be scrutinized. (Id. at 740.) The Declaration of Bryan Lamb shows that Defendant was negligent in pursuing his case, and as such, is not entitled to equitable relief. For example, Defendant states that the parties agreed that Mr. Jensen was hired to pursue settlement and attend a case management conference, not for all purposes. (Declaration of Bryan Lamb, par. 3.) Defendant also states that Mr. Jensen and Defendant were unable to agree on a time to meet and communications broke down when Defendant was unable to receive calls due to failure to pay his phone bill. (Id., par. 6.) Once communications broke down, Lamb "assumed that [Mr. Jensen] had persuaded the other side not to pursue the case, and they had dropped out." (Id., par. 7.) Defendant also states that he received some letters from Mr. Jensen but does not recall what they stated. (Id.) The evidence shows that Defendant did not take the necessary steps to ensure his case was being properly handled, and therefore, Defendant is not entitled to equitable relief. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: KING SECURITY SERVICES, INC. VS. SAN FRANCISCO CULTURAL ARTS TRADITIONS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, February 6, 2015, Line 7, DEFENDANT JAMES SOWERS MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND. The motion is denied. Plaintiff demonstrates that defendant was properly served. Actual physical delivery of process is not necessary when a defendant is being recalcitrant. Cases recognize the sufficiency of attempted delivery in the presence of a defendant who refuses to accept the papers. See In re Ball, (1934) 2 Cal.App.2d 578, 579; Trujillo v. Trujillo, (1945) 71 Cal.App.2d 257, 260. Defendant fails to demonstrate that the default was entered as the result of mistake, surprise, inadvertence, or excusable neglect. Defendant ignored the papers. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: CEM BUILDERS, INC. DBA TUCKER ENGINEERING VS. S.J. AMOROSO CONSTRUCTION CO. INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: City And County Of San Franciscos Demurrer To The Complaint Of Sj Amoroso Construction Company, Inc.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, February 6, 2015, Line 11, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO City And County Of San Franciscos Demurrer To The Complaint Of Sj Amoroso Construction Company, Inc. The demurrer to the second cause of action for equitable indemnity and the third cause of action for contribution is sustained without leave to amend. The complaint alleges breach of contractual duties and does not state a cause of action for negligence against Defendant. Further, Plaintiff admits in its opposition that "[e]ach of the obligations that the City has breached are founded upon the written contract for the Project." (Plaintiff's opposition brief, page 13.) Implied contractual indemnity is not available when the parties have a contract that contains an express indemnity clause. For example, in Maryland Casualty Co. v. Bailey and Sons, Inc. (1995) 35 Cal.App.4th 856, 873, the court held: "Where parties expressly contract with respect to the duty to indemnify, the extent of that duty must be determined from the contract and not by reliance on the independent doctrine of equitable indemnity. This rule seeks to permit people to voluntarily order their affairs in a manner agreeable to them and recognizes that equity rarely interferes with a contract knowledgeably executed." Other cases have reached the same conclusion. (See, e.g. Prince v. PG&E (2009) 45 Cal.4th 1151, 1158, "Express indemnity generally is not subject to equitable considerations or a joint legal obligation to the injured party; rather, it is enforced in accordance with the terms of the contracting parties' agreement; Markley v. Beagle (1967) 66 Cal.2d 951, 961, "Since the parties expressly contracted with respect to the contractors' duty to indemnify the owners, the extent of that duty must be determined from the contract and not from the independent doctrine of equitable indemnity.") Here, the contract entered into by Plaintiff and Defendant, attached to the complaint, contains an express indemnity provision at section 74. Thus, the equitable indemnity cause of action fails as a matter of law. The cases cited by Plaintiff in opposition, including E.L. White, Inc. v. Huntington Beach (1978) 21 Cal.3d 497, apply equitable indemnity principles when there is a tort cause of action, and therefore, do not apply to the case at hand. The complaint also does not state a cause of action for contribution, as there are no allegations that Defendant is a joint tortfeasor. (See Western Steamship Lines, Inc. v. San Pedro Peninsula Hosp. (1994) 8 Cal.4th 100, 108, fn. 6.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)
